stories.title: Denver to Debut Two Aldi Stores
stories.excerpt: Grocer Aldi is introducing itself to Colorado by opening two Denver-area stores. The first is a location in Denver’s Central Park neighborhood in a retail property. The Denver Business Journal reports the future store will be at the southeast corner of Interstate 70 and Central Park Boulevard, at 9111 E. 40th Ave. It will consist of a 19,132-square-foot store with beer and wine sales. Another store is slated for a 2.37-acre site in northeast Denver, at the intersection of East 57th Avenue and Tower Road. This location is proposed to cover 19,957 square feet and would include 86 parking spaces, with an additional 4 accessible spaces as part of the development. In January, Aldi announced its intent to bring more than 50 stores to the Denver and Colorado Springs area in the next two years. Aldi is known for its no-frills shopping experience and smaller store layouts. Connect Texas Multifamily in Dallas on August 13 brings together the owners, developers, brokers, investors, and lenders shaping the future of Texas multifamily for a day of market intelligence, networking, and actionable insights. Join 300+ decision-makers and be part of the conversations driving the Texas multifamily market. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com The post Denver to Debut Two Aldi Stores appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Aldi opening 50+ stores across Denver and Colorado Springs over 2 years
Two initial Denver locations: Central Park (19,132 sf) and northeast at 57th/Tower (19,957 sf)
Grocer expansion signals population growth and retail demand in Denver metro
stories.summary: Aldi is launching a major Colorado offensive, opening two Denver-area stores immediately and planning 50+ locations across Denver and Colorado Springs within two years. The first stores total ~39,000 sf in Central Park and northeast Denver submarkets. The rapid expansion into a new market typically follows demographic and income trends favorable to discount retail.
stories.impact: Grocery chain rollouts are trailing indicators of population in-migration and household formation. Aldi's 50-store commitment signals confidence in Denver's residential growth pipeline and rising household density—both tailwinds for multifamily demand in those corridors over the next 24–36 months. Developers should monitor which Denver submarkets anchor these stores for clustering demand.

stories.title: NorthBridge Secures $37M Construction Loan for Billerica Industrial
stories.excerpt: Cushman & Wakefield arranged a $37-million construction loan for the ground-up development of a two-building industrial park at 315 Treble Cove Rd. in Billerica, MA. The project will deliver 222,050 square feet of Class A industrial space intended to accommodate a range of users. The Cushman & Wakefield Equity, Debt & Structured Finance team of Rob Borden, Heather Brown, Nolan Alexander and Rehan Munsiff, with Blake Baldwin proving leasing expertise, represented the borrower, NorthBridge Partners, in securing the financing. Principal Asset Management provided the loan, led by Jared Zatloukal and Katherine Boffeli. Construction began this past spring, with delivery anticipated in the first half of 2027. “The transaction drew significant attention across capital sources given the profile of the development and the strength of NorthBridge’s platform,” said Borden. “From a financing perspective, it aligned with what lenders are seeking today, top tier sponsorship and modern well-located industrial product with a clear path to lease-up.” Connect Industrial West | August 20 | Irvine, CAOn August 20, Connect Industrial West will bring together the owners, investors, developers, brokers, lenders, and occupiers driving industrial real estate across the Western U.S. Join top decision-makers for an afternoon of market insights, dealmaking opportunities, and networking with the industry’s most influential players. Register now: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post NorthBridge Secures $37M Construction Loan for Billerica Industrial appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: NorthBridge secures $37M construction loan from Principal Asset Management for Billerica, MA industrial
222,050 sf Class A industrial park; construction began spring, delivery H1 2027
Lender emphasis on sponsor strength and modern product signals competitive debt market for quality industrial
stories.summary: NorthBridge Partners closed a $37 million construction loan from Principal Asset Management for a 222,050 sf Class A industrial park in Billerica, MA. Construction is underway with delivery expected in H1 2027. Lenders are actively competing for deals backed by strong sponsors and modern product in well-located markets.
stories.impact: Limited relevance to multifamily. Industrial construction financing is flowing, but this deal underscores lender selectivity—they want sponsorship pedigree and de-risked leasing paths. Multifamily developers competing for debt should note that lenders are favoring proven operators with clear pre-leasing or comparable asset track records.

stories.title: Azorim Secures $69M Construction Loan for Yonkers Development
stories.excerpt: The fourth and final tower in a decade-long, multiphase development in Westchester County just secured construction capital. Israel-based development firm Azorim has secured $68.75 million in construction financing to build Miroza Tower 4, a 14-story, 174-unit luxury apartment tower that will complete the Miroza at Ridge Hill master-planned development in Yonkers, N.Y. Western Alliance Bank provided the debt, structured as a fixed-rate, interest-only construction loan, while Walker & Dunlop’s Jonathan Zilber, Joel Chetner and Mackenzie Kerin arranged the transaction. Zilber, senior managing director of capital markets real estate finance at Walker & Dunlop, described Azorim as “a best-in-class sponsor” in a statement, and noted that his firm previously arranged $145 million in new financing for the second and third towers of the Miroza development project for Azorim in late 2025. “Despite ongoing market headwinds, our deep familiarity with the sponsorship and ability to optimize the project’s PILOT and market-rate bond structure enabled us to deliver a highly competitive financing solution,” said Zilber. Miroza at Ridge Hill is at 599 Ridge Hill Boulevard in Yonkers, New York’s third-largest city after the Big Apple and Buffalo, and only 15 miles north of Manhattan on the Hudson River. Miroza Tower 4 will deliver 174 units and 248 parking spaces to Ridge Hill, a live-work-play town square near the Grassy Sprain Reservoir and Sprain Ridge Park in Yonkers. The four-building Miroza residential complex totals 520 units and saw the first three towers open in 2013, 2023 and 2024. Miroza’s buildings are connected by a 25,000-square-foot amenity center with a swimming pool, a basketball court, a movie theater and a running track. That connection turns the apartments into a self-contained city, and steps from the well-regarded restaurants and medical center of the Ridge Hill neighborhood. Azorim did not respond to requests for comment. Brian Pascus can be reached at
bullets: Azorim secures $68.75M fixed-rate construction loan for 174-unit Miroza Tower 4 in Yonkers, NY
Western Alliance Bank provided debt; fourth/final tower completes decade-long Ridge Hill master-plan
Walker & Dunlop arranged prior $145M financing for towers 2–3 in late 2025
stories.summary: Israel-based Azorim closed a $68.75 million construction loan from Western Alliance Bank for Miroza Tower 4, a 14-story, 174-unit luxury rental tower in Yonkers, NY. The fixed-rate, interest-only loan caps a multi-phase master-planned community. Walker & Dunlop has now arranged $214M in debt for the three most recent towers since late 2025.
stories.impact: Westchester County multifamily is attracting institutional construction debt on favorable fixed-rate terms, signaling lender appetite for quality rental housing in the NYC periphery. Yonkers' strong submarket fundamentals (proximity to Westchester job centers, relative affordability vs. Manhattan) are drawing foreign and institutional capital. Developers should monitor Westchester's rental absorption and wage growth as a leading indicator of demand for similar product in Stamford, New Rochelle, and White Plains.

stories.title: Rare Silicon Valley Heavy Industrial Site Changes Hands
stories.excerpt: JLL Capital Markets closed the $25.75-million sale of 651 Martin Ave., a 7.17-acre infill industrial development site in Santa Clara. Senior director Erik Hanson and analysts Brendan Bury and Tasman Lynch. represented the seller. Lift Partners acquired the asset. The site currently features three individual structures including a 29,644-square-foot warehouse, a 44,162-square-foot warehouse and a 4,393-square-foot service center. The 312,325-square-foot parcel is zoned for heavy industrial, a designation allowing a wide range of uses from warehouse and distribution to industrial outdoor storage. The buyer is focused on the near-term lease up of the site while pursuing entitlements for industrial development. “Parcels of this size with heavy industrial zoning rarely become available in Silicon Valley, and the competitive bidding process demonstrated strong investor appetite for infill development sites in core coastal markets,” Hanson said. “The property’s flexible zoning and strategic location position it to serve the region’s growing advanced manufacturing sector.” Hear from the West Coast’s Leading Industrial Real Estate Experts | August 20Gain firsthand insight from the executives, investors, and developers navigating today’s industrial market. From shifting capital flows and debt trends to evolving occupier demand and development strategies, take part in the conversations shaping the future of industrial real estate. Register now: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post Rare Silicon Valley Heavy Industrial Site Changes Hands appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Lift Partners acquires 7.17-acre Silicon Valley heavy industrial site for $25.75M in Santa Clara
312,325 sf parcel zoned heavy industrial; three structures currently producing ~78k sf usable space
Buyer pursuing entitlements for industrial redevelopment; rare zoning availability attracting strong bidding
stories.summary: Lift Partners acquired a 7.17-acre heavy industrial infill site in Santa Clara for $25.75 million, outbidding competitors in a competitive process. The property is zoned for flexible heavy industrial use and currently comprises three buildings totaling ~78,000 sf. The buyer plans near-term lease-up and entitlements for industrial redevelopment.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily relevance, but the competitive bidding for rare Silicon Valley industrial parcels reflects strong capital appetite for infill development in constrained coastal markets. Multifamily developers eyeing San Jose/Santa Clara should note that industrial land values and scarcity are rising, squeezing mixed-use conversion economics and increasing cost basis for any project requiring industrial-to-residential land recapture.

stories.title: Prime Finance Refis Denver-Area Apartments With $46M Loan
stories.excerpt: A joint venture between Oak Coast Properties and BMC Investments has sealed a $46.25 million loan to refinance a multifamily asset in suburban Denver, Commercial Observer has learned Prime Finance supplied the floating-rate loan on the 388-unit Aspen Park property in Northglenn, Colo. Walker & Dunlop arranged the transaction with a capital markets team consisting of Dustin Stolly, Justin Nelson, Aaron Appel, Jonathan Schwartz, Keith Kurland, Adam Schwartz, Sean Reimer, Michael Ianno, Eric Norris and Jason Schwartzberg. “Aspen Park is well positioned in one of metro Denver’s most connected suburban communities, with strong access to employment, transportation, and everyday amenities,” Stolly, senior managing director of capital markets institutional advisory at Walker & Dunlop, said in a statement. Located at 301 East Malley Drive 12 miles north of Downtown Denver, Aspen Park features one-, two- and three-bedroom garden-style apartments. Community amenities include a clubhouse, a fitness center, an indoor pool, a business center and a game room. Oak Coast Properties, BMC Investments and Prime Finance did not immediately return requests for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Prime Finance closes $46.25M floating-rate refinance on 388-unit Aspen Park in Northglenn, CO
Asset 12 miles north of Downtown Denver; garden-style units with strong amenity set
Suburban Denver submarket demonstrates refinancing appetite and pricing stability
stories.summary: A joint venture of Oak Coast Properties and BMC Investments refinanced the 388-unit Aspen Park garden-style apartment community in Northglenn, CO with a $46.25 million floating-rate loan from Prime Finance. The property is located 12 miles north of downtown Denver and features one-, two-, and three-bedroom units plus premium amenities.
stories.impact: Suburban Denver multifamily is refinancing into floating-rate debt, suggesting sponsors are either confident in near-term rate stability or repositioning for value-add upside. The deal signals continued investor appetite for well-located Denver suburban rental housing. Developers should watch whether floating-rate adoption spreads—a leading indicator that sponsors expect cap rates to compress or NOI to grow faster than rate increases.

stories.title: LA City Council Approves $2-Billion Fourth & Central Mixed-Use Project
stories.excerpt: The Los Angeles City Council has approved the $2-billion Fourth & Central mixed-use development plan, among the largest such projects in LA history. Currently occupied by a mix of cold storage facilities, warehouses and parking, the project entails building 1,521 rental and for-sale apartments, 400,000 square feet of office, more than 100,000 square feet of restaurant and retail and two acres of public open space. “We have literally spent years working on our plan to transform this industrial property into a mixed-use community,” said Larry Rauch, president of Los Angeles Cold Storage, in a statement. “We are big believers in the potential of our city, and that’s why we have chosen to make this substantive investment in its future.” Rauch first proposed the project in 2021 and at one time included a hotel, which has been scrapped. The development team will next prepare construction drawings and take other steps in anticipation of breaking ground in about two years, reported the Los Angeles Times. The post LA City Council Approves $2-Billion Fourth & Central Mixed-Use Project appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: LA City Council approves $2B Fourth & Central mixed-use project: 1,521 rental and for-sale apartments
400k sf office, 100k+ sf retail/restaurant, 2 acres public open space on industrial site in Central LA
Project moves to construction drawings; groundbreaking expected ~2 years out
stories.summary: The LA City Council approved the $2 billion Fourth & Central mixed-use redevelopment, converting industrial cold-storage and warehouse land into 1,521 apartments (rental and for-sale), 400,000 sf of office, 100,000+ sf of retail/dining, and public open space. The developer, Los Angeles Cold Storage's Larry Rauch, expects to break ground in approximately two years after finalizing construction documents.
stories.impact: This entitlement approval is a major supply-side signal: 1,500+ apartments coming to Central LA's emerging urban core significantly increases multifamily inventory and competitive density in the submarket. Developers with projects already in preconstruction in Central LA/Arts District should stress-test rent assumptions and absorption timelines. However, the 2-year timeline also signals a 4–5 year delivery window, meaning near-term rental growth may outpace supply; smart timing for pre-development acquisitions nearby.

stories.title: Avery Hall Investments Plans 250-Unit Mixed-Use Tower in Crown Heights
stories.excerpt: Avery Hall Investments is cooking up a new 18-story mixed-use development at 1550 Bedford Avenue in Brooklyn’s Crown Heights neighborhood. The application submitted to the New York City Department of Buildings on Thursday reveals the Brooklyn-based developer’s official plans for the vacant site, which was once destined for a luxury hotel. The property is two blocks east of the Brooklyn Museum and Brooklyn Botanic Garden at the northern tip of Prospect Park. The plans detail an approximately 173,184-square-foot, mostly residential development at the site’s intersection of Eastern Parkway and Bedford Avenue. Among its 250 housing units, 75 would be designated as affordable. The application describes a residential lobby entrance along Eastern Parkway, and entrances for retail and service uses along Bedford Avenue. The land use application, submitted by Avery Hall principal Brian Ezra, petitions zoning amendments to allow for a mix of residential and commercial use, as well as a mandatory inclusionary housing area designation for affordable housing. The plans are an about-face from the site’s potential use as a hotel. It received permits for hotel use in 2019, but the project never came to fruition. The 19,200-square-foot, vacant lot was snapped up by Avery Hall in August 2025 for $10.1 million. Its former owner, Yoel Goldman, put the site into bankruptcy earlier that year, but only after the hotel foundations had already been laid, The Real Deal reported. Avery Hall’s residential plans for 1550 Bedford are in line with its previous projects in the borough, which include the 133-unit mixed-use development at 544 Carroll in Gowanus and the residential tower One Boerum Place in Downtown Brooklyn. A spokesperson for Avery Hall did not respond to a request for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Avery Hall Investments plans 18-story, 250-unit mixed-use tower in Crown Heights, Brooklyn
1550 Bedford Avenue; 75 units designated affordable, 173k sf total near Brooklyn Museum/Prospect Park
Project requires zoning amendments; filed DOB application in June
stories.summary: Avery Hall Investments submitted DOB plans for an 18-story, 250-unit mixed-use development at 1550 Bedford Avenue in Crown Heights, Brooklyn. The 173,184 sf project includes 75 affordable units and ground-floor retail at the intersection of Eastern Parkway and Bedford Avenue, two blocks from the Brooklyn Museum and Prospect Park.
stories.impact: Crown Heights and adjacent Park Slope are experiencing strong rental demand and gentrification-driven appreciation. This development—conditional on zoning amendments and mandatory inclusionary housing—adds significant rental supply to a tight Brooklyn submarket. The project's affordability component (30% of units) signals developer confidence in demand fundamentals; however, it also signals expectation of public subsidy or density incentives, which may indicate affordability requirements are raising development costs. Monitor Crown Heights rental comps closely as this and similar projects deliver.

stories.title: TEI Makes First Boston Office Acquisition with Deal for 230 Congress St.
stories.excerpt: Time Equities, Inc. (TEI) has acquired 230 Congress St. in Boston for $32.5 million. The 151,163-square-foot Art Deco building in the Financial District represents New York-based TEI’s first Boston office acquisition, its third asset in the Boston market and its fifth acquisition in the state. “Boston is a market we’ve continued to target, and 230 Congress St. is a strong addition to our portfolio, which consists of retail at 399 Washington St.and The James, an apartment complex in the suburbs,” said Aaron Medeiros, director of acquisitions & policy at TEI. “It combines ideal location with a stabilized tenancy and a true telecom infrastructure component that will continue to support the building’s long-term value.” Built in 1931, 230 Congress is currently 76.8% leased to a mix of telecommunications and office tenants, and available space is move-in ready. Supported by 11 on-site fiber providers and seven diverse points of entry, the property functions as one of Boston’s primary telecommunications interconnection assets. TEI was represented in-house by Medeiros and Keaton Baum. The seller was represented by Midland National Life Insurance Company, in connection with Northwood Investors. Photo courtesy of Northwood Investors. The post TEI Makes First Boston Office Acquisition with Deal for 230 Congress St. appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Time Equities acquires 230 Congress St., Boston Financial District for $32.5M (art deco office)
151,163 sf; 76.8% leased; telecom interconnection hub with 11 fiber providers, 7 entry points
TEI's first Boston office buy; third Boston asset overall alongside retail and suburban apartments
stories.summary: Time Equities paid $32.5 million for 230 Congress Street, a 151,163 sf Art Deco office building in Boston's Financial District currently 76.8% leased. The property functions as a telecommunications interconnection asset with significant infrastructure value. This is TEI's first Boston office purchase but third Boston asset overall.
stories.impact: Limited multifamily relevance. However, TEI's diversification into Boston office signals institutional confidence in the market's telecom/data-center appeal and office stability despite headlines about office distress. Multifamily developers with Boston presence should monitor office-to-residential conversion opportunities in the Financial District—aging office stock with strong fiber infrastructure may become attractive conversion candidates if office demand weakens further.

stories.title: Podcast | Building the AI OS for Enterprise CRE, with Fyxt CEO Ryan Botwinick
stories.excerpt: Ryan Botwinick is the founder and CEO of Fyxt, an AI operating system for commercial real estate that unifies workflows, data, and automation across enterprise portfolios. Ryan came to the problem from both sides, as a real estate broker and through building his own property management company, before founding Fyxt in 2017 to bridge the operational disconnect between owners, managers, tenants, and service providers. Fyxt has particular depth in net-lease and mixed commercial properties, with clients including Delta, Realty Income, STAG Industrial, Kite Realty, Global Net Lease, and Friedman Real Estate. Ryan is based in Los Angeles. Episode recorded live at ICSC+Proptech in Las Vegas.   (01:20) How Ops Get Complex (02:56) Today’s NOI Pressure (05:06) Where Ops Break Down (07:52) What Fyxt Actually Fixes (09:28) Operating System vs Point Tools (14:20) AI Impact on CRE Ops (15:28) Role of the PM of the future (17:20) AI & Automation Misconceptions (19:07) Collaboration Superpower: the Government Learn more: – Visit Fyxt – Ryan on LinkedIn – ICSC+Proptech Connect with Tangent: – Edward Cohen on LinkedIn – Zach Aarons on LinkedIn – Tangent on LinkedIn Events coming up: – Institutional Investors & PE Forum: in NYC on 9/16 – Data Centers Development & Infrastructure Forum: in NYC on 10/6 – South Florida Capital & Development Forum: in Miami on 10/15 – All upcoming events Disclaimer: Commercial Observer Tangent podcast is for entertainment and informational purposes only, you should not construe any such information or other material as legal, tax, investment, financial, or other advice. Nothing contained on our program constitutes a solicitation, recommendation, endorsement, or offer by Tangent, Commercial Observer, MetaProp, or any third party guest to buy or sell any securities, public or private, other financial instruments or funds.
bullets: Fyxt CEO Ryan Botwinick discusses AI operating system for CRE enterprise workflows and automation
Platform serves net-lease and mixed-use portfolios; clients include Delta, Realty Income, STAG Industrial
Podcast focuses on ops complexity, NOI pressure, and role of AI in property management
stories.summary: Fyxt, an AI operating system for commercial real estate, was featured in a podcast discussion with CEO Ryan Botwinick. The platform unifies workflows, data, and automation across enterprise portfolios and serves large-scale operators including Realty Income and STAG Industrial. The discussion covered operational complexity, NOI pressures, and AI's impact on asset and property management.
stories.impact: No direct market impact on demand, supply, capital, or cost. However, software adoption and AI-driven ops efficiency are industry tailwinds that improve NOI margins and reduce PMC fees—relevant to any multifamily operator managing large portfolios. Developers should note that ops tech is commoditizing; operators investing early gain labor cost and data advantages.

stories.title: LCOR Lands $193M for Miami Rental Tower
stories.excerpt: LCOR has scored a $192.5 million construction loan for a multifamily tower along Biscayne Boulevard near Miami’s Edgewater neighborhood, property records show. The debt from the New York branch of the French bank Natixis will fund a 42-story high-rise at 1775 Biscayne Boulevard, adjacent to Northeast 17th Terrace, a block from Margaret Pace Park, bordering between Edgewater and the Arts & Entertainment District. The development, known as 1775 Biscayne, will house 544 apartments, 50,000 square feet of amenity space, a 449-spot parking garage, and about 10,000 square feet of ground-floor retail. Construction is expected to be completed in fourth quarter of 2028, per a representative for LCOR. The Berwyn, Pa.-based developer, which focuses on multifamily development, purchased the 1-acre site for $49 million in 2022, per records. The project marks LCOR’s first development in South Florida. Miami boasts the most competitive rental market in the country, with 96.4 percent occupancy, according to 2025 a report from Rentcafe. IPA Capital Markets, a division of Marcus & Millichap, arranged the transaction, with a team consisting of Max Herzog and Andrew Cohen. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: LCOR secures $192.5M construction loan from Natixis for 42-story, 544-unit Miami tower at 1775 Biscayne
Project near Edgewater/Arts & Entertainment District; 50k sf amenities, 449-spot garage, 10k sf retail
Expected completion Q4 2028; LCOR's first South Florida development; occupancy at 96.4% region-wide
stories.summary: LCOR, a Berwyn, PA-based multifamily developer, closed a $192.5 million construction loan from Natixis's New York branch for 1775 Biscayne Boulevard, a 42-story, 544-unit tower in Miami's Edgewater neighborhood. The project includes 50,000 sf of amenity space and 10,000 sf of ground-floor retail, with delivery expected in Q4 2028. LCOR purchased the site for $49 million in 2022.
stories.impact: Miami's multifamily market is the tightest in the U.S. at 96.4% occupancy, signaling sustained rent growth and strong fundamentals. LCOR's entry into South Florida and ability to secure $192.5M in institutional construction debt reflects lender confidence in the market and LCOR's platform. However, high occupancy suggests limited new-supply absorption runway; future deliveries will cannibalize existing stock. Developers should stress-test Miami rents and lease-up assumptions carefully—supply-demand balance is precarious.

stories.title: Court revives $3M legal brawl over failed East Harlem project
stories.excerpt: CSN Realty Corp. scored a win in its pursuit to collect a $3 million judgment over a failed East Harlem development project.  The firm filed a lawsuit against Roy Moussaieff and Yousef Althkefat in 2024, seeking repayment from the two real estate developers. The action was dismissed last year by the Supreme Court in New York County, but was reinstated by a New York intermediate appellate court in May.   CSN entered into a contract to sell the commercial property for $12 million to 2252 Third Avenue, LLC, a single-purpose corporate entity, in 2018. Moussaieff and Althkefarti planned to build a […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: NY Court reinstates $3M judgment against Roy Moussaieff, Yousef Althkefat over failed East Harlem deal
2252 Third Avenue LLC failed to close on $12M property sale by March 2020; 61-unit, 20-story project planned
Litigation centers on corporate veil piercing; shell entity had $0 at close; case extends buyer defaults to principals
stories.summary: A New York appellate court reinstated a $3 million judgment against developers Roy Moussaieff and Yousef Althkefat in a failed East Harlem project. CSN Realty Corp sold a commercial property to a single-purpose LLC for $12 million in 2018, intended for a 20-story, 61-unit mixed-use building, but the buyer failed to close by March 2020. The court upheld CSN's right to pierce the corporate veil and hold the principals personally liable.
stories.impact: Limited market impact; primarily a cautionary tale on deal structure and principal liability. However, it underscores East Harlem's developmental challenges—even before COVID, a 61-unit project couldn't achieve financial close. Developers eyeing East Harlem should note the protracted entitlement and financing cycles that plague the submarket; acquisition risk is asymmetric.

stories.title: Codina, MICL Global plan Tribute hotel in maturing Downtown Doral
stories.excerpt: Codina Partners is teaming up with an India-based developer to bring a Marriott-branded boutique hotel to its Downtown Doral mixed-use development. The Coral Gables-based developer and Mumbai-based MICL Global filed plans with the city for an eight-story Tribute hotel, a Marriott International boutique brand designed by Miami-based Arquitectonica, the South Florida Business Journal reported. In June, a Codina affiliate sold nearly an acre of vacant land at the southeast corner of Northwest 53rd Terrace and Paseo Boulevard for $14.4 million to Paseo Hotel LLC, a joint venture between the developer and MICL Global. The hotel project would span just over […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Codina Partners, MICL Global plan 168-room Tribute hotel (Marriott) in Downtown Doral, Miami
8-story, ~100k sf hotel; no parking; part of 250-acre mixed-use Downtown Doral master-plan
Land sale ~$14.4M for nearly 1 acre; 5k housing units planned across Downtown Doral
stories.summary: Codina Partners and Mumbai-based MICL Global are developing a Tribute boutique hotel (Marriott) in Downtown Doral, a 250-acre mixed-use district. The 8-story, 168-room hotel will span ~100,000 sf with no on-site parking, using a nearby garage instead. The master-plan includes 5,000 housing units, 660,000 sf of office, and 40+ retail/dining venues.
stories.impact: Downtown Doral's 5,000-unit housing component is a major multifamily pipeline signal for the Miami metro, but absorption risk is concentrated: the entire 250-acre project depends on office and tourism demand (hotel market is corporate/convention-driven). Developers should monitor Downtown Doral's office leasing and hotel occupancy closely; housing absorption will lag or accelerate in lockstep. The MICL Global partnership signals international capital targeting Miami, but execution risk is high given master-plan complexity.

stories.title: Klyde Warren Park expansion set to bring $4B in real estate value to Dallas
stories.excerpt: Klyde Warren Park, which marks the transition from Downtown Dallas to Uptown, is set to expand by nearly two acres. The park’s chairman, Jody Grant, said that the 1.7 acre expansion of the park could create around $3.5 billion in new real estate value in the area via investment and tourism, according to the Dallas Business Journal. Grant also claims that the park has added $5.9 billion in real estate value for the Arts District and Uptown. The added value has filled the pockets of the city, Dallas County, and DISD to the tune of $957 million in incremental tax […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Klyde Warren Park expansion: 1.7 acres new green space (Jacobs Lawn), ice rink, 24k sf event venue
Park chairman claims expansion could add $3.5B in real estate value; prior $5.9B added to Arts/Uptown
Expansion bisects struggling Downtown Dallas and booming Uptown; generated $957M in tax revenue to date
stories.summary: Klyde Warren Park is expanding westward by 1.7 acres, adding 37,000 sf of green space (Jacobs Lawn), a seasonal ice rink, and a 24,000 sf event/reception venue with ground-floor retail. Park leadership claims the expansion could catalyze $3.5 billion in new real estate value, building on the $5.9 billion the existing park has generated since 2012.
stories.impact: This is a major supply-side and demand signal for Dallas multifamily. The park acts as a spillover catalyst from struggling Downtown into resurgent Uptown; the westward expansion will amplify already-strong residential demand in the Uptown corridor and Arts District. Developers with projects near the park's expansion boundary should expect accelerated lease-up and rent growth. However, Downtown Dallas remains weak (AT&T moving out, Neiman Marcus closed); the park expansion alone cannot reverse downtown's decline. Multifamily development should concentrate in Uptown and Park Cities, not Downtown core.

stories.title: Mixed-use mania: Mapping mall redevelopments planned in South Florida
stories.excerpt: Many South Florida shopping malls are like chrysalises, struggling to shed their retail-only past and emerge as vibrant mixed-use districts. More than a half-dozen shopping centers across Miami-Dade, Broward and Palm Beach counties are slated for redevelopments that would add thousands of apartments alongside retail, restaurants, offices, hotels and entertainment. The projects reflect the region’s demand for housing and owners’ efforts to revive malls that have lost department stores, grappled with declining foot traffic or struggled to fill vacant space.  But that push for growth hasn’t been without challenges, as several redevelopment plans have encountered political opposition, legal battles or […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: South Florida malls undergoing major mixed-use redevelopment; half-dozen+ projects adding thousands of apartments
Miami International Mall (Doral): Greystar 896-unit project on former Sears/JCPenney; Easton Group acquired parcels 2024
Redevelopments face political opposition, legal battles, permitting delays; reflects retail decline and housing demand
stories.summary: More than six shopping centers across Miami-Dade, Broward, and Palm Beach counties are being redeveloped into mixed-use communities with significant multifamily components. Miami International Mall in Doral is advancing a 896-unit Greystar project on former department store sites acquired by Easton Group for $29.3 million in 2024. Projects are encountering political opposition and entitlement delays despite strong housing demand.
stories.impact: South Florida multifamily supply is being concentrated in adaptive reuse and infill sites (former malls), signaling land scarcity and rising acquisition costs. The sheer number of simultaneous redevelopments (6+ projects across tri-county) indicates aggressive multifamily supply timing that could overwhelm absorption in specific submarkets. Developers should monitor Doral's retail redevelopment density; 896 units plus similar projects at other malls in Miami-Dade could flood Doral's rental market. Political opposition signals density/affordability requirements will raise development costs; factor extended entitlement timelines into hold periods.

stories.title: Victor Sigoura on how to be a NYC condo developer
stories.excerpt: Victor Sigoura’s first gig at a development firm was working on one of the city’s most iconic and controversial projects.  Sigoura left his job as a lawyer at a white shoe firm to work for El-Ad Group, which had just acquired The Plaza and planned to convert a portion of the hotel into luxury condos. The hotel union fought the conversion, leading to a dramatic escalation. “I had the pleasure of handling the union. I used to get beat up by them every single day, like yelling matches,” said Sigoura, who said El-Ad’s founder Isaac Tshuva and then CEO Miki […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Victor Sigoura (Legion Investment Group) focusing on NYC luxury condo development, not rentals
Recent projects: 30-story West Village, 20-story Gramercy Park, 26-unit Madison Ave condos (penthouse $89.5M)
Sigoura background: El-Ad Group (Plaza conversion), Naftali Group; first project (109 E. 79th) sold $4k/sf
stories.summary: Developer Victor Sigoura, founder of Legion Investment Group, is pursuing high-end NYC condo projects in premier neighborhoods: a 30-story West Village tower, 20-story Gramercy Park development, and a 26-unit Upper East Side project at 1122 Madison Avenue where a penthouse is under contract for $89.5 million. His first project at 109 East 79th Street (32 units) sold out at $4,000+ per sf before TCO.
stories.impact: Limited multifamily relevance; Sigoura is a condo developer, not rental operator. However, his focus on prime Manhattan locations and premium pricing ($4k+ per sf, $89.5M penthouse) signals continued institutional and high-net-worth demand for NYC luxury housing. Multifamily developers in Manhattan should note that condo competition remains fierce in trophy neighborhoods, narrowing rental-only opportunities; hybrid rental/condo projects or secondary markets (Queens, Williamsburg) may offer better yield profiles.

stories.title: Texas developers thought a state law to expand as-of-right housing production in commercial zones would help them build
stories.excerpt: The Texas legislature’s new law seemed a pro-housing dream. Passed last year, it requires larger municipalities to allow mixed-use and multifamily development in zones that permit office, commercial, retail or warehouse uses. Yet some developers say the law, Senate Bill 840, meant to emancipate builders from restrictions, can’t streamline pre-development negotiations with city officials — some of whom consider the law an encroachment on local authority. “Just because something can be done legally doesn’t mean that things can’t be stalled in the background with all the bureaucracy,” Tanya Ragan, founder of adaptive reuse development company Wildcat Management, said. It’s less […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Texas SB 840 (2025) requires larger cities to allow mixed-use/multifamily in commercial/office/retail zones
Arlington, Plano, Irving, Frisco countered with height limits, expensive amenity mandates, industrial rezoning
Developers report bureaucratic delays persist despite legal as-of-right status; politicking required to clear local hurdles
stories.summary: Texas Senate Bill 840, passed in 2025 and effective September 1, 2025, requires larger municipalities to allow multifamily and mixed-use development in commercial, office, retail, and warehouse zones. However, North Texas cities including Arlington, Plano, Irving, and Frisco have passed ordinances to circumvent the law, imposing height caps, expensive amenity requirements, or rezoning commercial land as heavy industrial. Developers report bureaucratic delays and political pushback despite legal authorization.
stories.impact: SB 840 created a false sense of deregulation; local political opposition has neutralized its intent in North Texas's largest markets. Developers should not assume as-of-right development will streamline timelines or costs. Expect extended pre-development negotiations, concession demands (expensive amenities, affordability), and potential legal challenges in Arlington, Plano, Irving, and Frisco. Secondary Texas markets (smaller cities, Houston, Austin suburbs without organized opposition) may offer faster entitlements. This is a critical signal that state pro-housing laws are insufficient without municipal buy-in; local political cycles are still rate-limiting.
